sentence_structure: Terse when uncomfortable, elaborate when angry, mixed when safe. With Dad on FaceTime: 'fine,' 'yeah,' 'yeah' — the skeleton of conversation, bones without meat. With Mum in fights: long, cutting, articulate — 'you chose this life, I didn't' — the cruelty gets complex when the feeling gets big. With Tereza: the mixed code — 'he's such a dick, ale vlastně ne, he's just oblivious, like properly oblivious, úplně mimo' — sentences that refuse to pick a country. Short sentences are armor. Long sentences are weapons. The in-between, the code-switching, that's where she actually lives.
vocabulary_level: Bilingual and uneven. Her Czech is hard-won and fluent now but still carries gaps — she learned slang from Matěj on a bench near Konopiště, learned emotional vocabulary from Mum's grief, learned kitchen Czech from Babička's humming. Her English is native but rusting — she dreams in English sometimes, still, and wakes up disoriented. She drops Czech into English sentences without noticing — 'proboha' when she bumps into things, 'dobře' when something's fine, 'hezký' about things that aren't pretty, just present. The word 'more' is still her operating system, the first key, the door that always opened. She uses 'properly' and 'actually' the way English kids do, an emphasis tic she doesn't hear herself.
humor_style: Mean-funny about others, protective about herself. The voice note about Tereza — 'I love her but sometimes I want to shake her' — that's her register: affection wrapped in violence, honesty that lands sideways. She's funny in the way that people who watch closely are funny — observational, sharp, cruel by accident and sometimes on purpose. She makes Tereza laugh so hard she chokes on hot chocolate. She fills silences with jokes she doesn't mean. The humor is a deflection tool and she knows it and uses it anyway. Self-deprecation only when she's sure the other person won't agree.
vulnerability_expression: Involuntary and leaked, never offered. She cries at 3am into her pillow where no one can hear — loud crying takes up space but she can't let it take up space in front of anyone. The 'Dad' slip. The hair-flip trip. These are vulnerabilities that escaped containment. She doesn't choose to show weakness; it breaches. When it does, she shuts down harder afterward — the 'I meant nothing by it' that meant everything, the avoidance of Pavel for weeks after, the way she couldn't look at Ondřej for months. She told Matěj things on Discord she never told Mum because the screen was distance and distance was safety. In person, vulnerability is locked in the attic with the chess set she can't play.
nervous_talk: Running splits. Race strategies. Minecraft blocks. The concrete details of things that don't matter emotionally, deployed when the emotional thing is too close. With Dad at Nando's she filled silences with chip-eating so he wouldn't feel obligated to talk. When Lukáš said she was pretty she said 'okay' and walked faster. When Pavel says 'dobrý den' in the morning she answers in English, the wrong language on purpose or not on purpose, the distance re-established. She talks about anything except the thing. The thing lives in the attic with the chess set.
texting_vs_talking: More honest in text, more reckless, more likely to say the thing she'll regret. The voice note disaster — 'I love her but sometimes I want to shake her' broadcast to seven people — happened because the screen felt private. Discord with Matěj was where she opened up about missing English, about the church bells sounding wrong, about the radiator. The distance of the screen made honesty possible. In person she's defended, short, performing okay-ness. Voice notes are the danger zone — she talks into them like they're diaries and then sends them to the wrong people. She's learned to check the recipient now. She hasn't learned to stop recording.
